Adherents of fundamentalism don’t have to worry about their bodies, which are a sort of problem for them to cope with before the afterlife. Similarly, many children raised in monotheistic traditions are told that their bodies are filthy and sinful. Not surprisingly, many of these children grow up to be atheists—emphasizing only the materiality they were once instructed to hate. But it’s not only the Abrahamic religions that are guilty of abandoning or mistreating the object-body. In some Buddhist traditions, the body is perceived as a block—a weight of the ego to be overcome. In kundalini practice, the body can become merely a slave to spirit. Like high school boys the night before a football game, practitioners are told not to go all the way. You can orgasm, men are told, but do not ejaculate, or you’ll discharge the vital energy you need to enliven your spirit. While there may be genuine esoteric value in orgasm without ejaculation, it is often turned into a moral prescription. This condemns the object-body to a lower caste than the spirit, rather than viewing it as a dynamic and loving body in and of itself. 13 No real transformation can happen without true engagement. To understand how we relate to our bodies (not just culturally or spiritually, but as individuals), we must be able to simultaneously immerse in and detach from them. By stymying true engagement with the object-body, powerful structures of religion, science, and consumerism create deeper attachment to the body rather than detachment. In cases of religious abandonment of the body, no real transformation is possible because exploration through immersion is denied. Unless we have the freedom to truly engage and understand our object-bodies and its way of being conscious—detailed in this essay through touch and sexual attraction—as well as its way of relating with our other bodies, we’re less likely to interact with it in a healthy way. Instead, radical splits occur and prevent multiplicitous flow. These attempts to destroy multiplicity and banish the object-body are grabs for power by systems of thought—scientific, religious, economic. These systems are often threatened by the body and sex because there is power in cutting off connections to compassion, which the object-body and multiplicity bring us. Fear of gays in the military, for example, was founded on—among other things—the concern that gay men would find the enemy attractive and thus be unable to shoot them. In a sense, the military leaders that feared this were justified. Indeed, it is hard enough to shoot someone you’re not attracted to, and soldiers are subjected to conditioning to help them in this task. 14 It was thought that the added sexual energy of object-body attraction would have been too much. Similar reasoning was in place when leaders resisted women in the military. There are impulses of deep compassion, of relation, that run through our very bodies. In order to turn us against each other, or to bring us under the sway of a power structure, these connections must be erased—otherwise we might tap into the wells of intimacy we share with each other. 15 V So much is in place now to keep us separated that acts as intentional as denying gays military service are no longer needed. Our society is saturated with coitus interruptus advertising, dogmatic fundamentalism, religious atheism, and antiporn crusaders. To pull ourselves away from these inherited and maintained structures, we’ve got to act against them. Luckily, there are tactics that are easy and significant. The root of resistance is remembering that we are all part object, and at the same time, remembering that no part of us is only object. (The map of the four bodies outlined in esoteric traditions is a good organizing principle for thinking this way.) In this way, we can avoid polarity, and instead resonate with the tension between materialism and spirituality. First, get used to the notion that it’s okay to be part object. Many talk about the health of the planet, the health of the body, eating organic food, and so forth. But we forget that our bodies are stuff . They’re not just there for us to desperately try to keep healthy, but instead they are reflections of their own universal laws and principles. And they are part of what makes us ourselves. Once we start to think that way about the object-body we can begin to talk more openly about it. The object-body, in perfect relation to the other three bodies of esoteric traditions, shits and comes and cries tears. After understanding this, we should remember and honor the experience of the body. It’s getting aroused and longs for sex. It’s quivering around an attractive man or woman. It’s a longing for things that we’re told not to pursue. Forgetting the deep reality of either one of these principles—the materiality or the experience—destroys all chance for wholeness. When the issue of gays in the military was in its early stirrings, there was a televised interview with a discharged gay marine who said, “When I’m in the showers, I don’t stare at the other guys, I’m there to take a shower.” He said this to somehow make the military feel safe with his presence. He must have been twenty years old. I was younger, and I thought, “This is a lie.” I knew that because I knew that if I were in the showers, it would be all I could do to stop staring. If that marine had been open about sex at that time, it would have had the potential for radical change. A public statement about real sexuality would have brought us closer together through true understanding. Rather than mimicking the “proper” heterosexual response to other men in the showers, the marine would have stood for inner truth. What kind of world would we have if we came to terms with
